1:
"Now as touching things offered unto idols, we know that we all have knowledge. Knowledge puffeth up, but charity edifieth." The witnesses note the principle that knowledge puffs up but love builds up. Chrysostom notes the charge against the Corinthians — many, having learned that not the things which enter defile the man, partook freely of idol-feasts, abusing their knowledge to the harm of the weak. The witnesses note Paul taking up the question of things offered to idols, granting that all have knowledge, but laying down the great principle — knowledge puffs up, while charity (love) edifies; the contrast that governs the whole discussion — mere knowledge inflates with pride, but love builds up the brethren, so that knowledge without love is harmful, and love must guide the use of liberty; knowledge and love contrasted. The witnesses note we all have knowledge. The witnesses note knowledge puffeth up, charity edifieth.

The witnesses note knowledge puffs up but charity edifies — the principle governing the whole discussion of idol-meat and liberty.

So the witnesses make the verse the principle of knowledge and love. The lesson the expositors draw is that knowledge without love inflates with pride, but love builds up, and love must guide the use of liberty. The witnesses note the subject: "things offered unto idols" — Paul takes up the question of idol-meat (meat sacrificed to idols, then sold or eaten at feasts); the subject; the idol-meat; the question; the matter at hand; the idol-offerings. The expositors observe the granting: "we know that we all have knowledge" — Paul grants that all (the Corinthians) have knowledge (that an idol is nothing); the granting; the knowledge admitted; the all have knowledge; the concession; the shared knowledge. The witnesses press the principle: "knowledge puffeth up, but charity edifieth" — knowledge puffs up (inflates with pride), but charity (love) edifies (builds up); the principle; the contrast; the knowledge and love; the puffing and the building; the governing rule. The expositors draw the lesson on the contrast: mere knowledge, without love, inflates with pride — making one conceited, contemptuous of the weak; but love builds up the brethren; the contrast; the puffing up; the edifying; the pride and the love; the two effects. The witnesses observe Chrysostom's note on the abuse: Chrysostom notes the Corinthians abused their knowledge — partaking freely of idol-feasts, harming the weak; the abuse; the knowledge misused; the harm to the weak; the liberty abused; the pride. The expositors note the governing principle: this principle (knowledge puffs up, love edifies) governs the whole discussion — the use of liberty must be guided by love, not mere knowledge; the governing principle; the love guiding; the liberty bounded; the knowledge subordinated; the rule of love. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we all have knowledge, the knowledge puffeth up, charity edifieth, and the principle of love. The verse thus records the principle of knowledge and love — teaching that knowledge without love inflates with pride, but love builds up, and love must guide the use of liberty.
2:
"And if any man think that he knoweth any thing, he knoweth nothing yet as he ought to know." The witnesses note the rebuke of conceited knowledge. Gill notes whoever has an opinion of himself, or is conceited with his own knowledge, fancying he knows more than he does — which is the case of those elated with knowledge, who treat others with contempt and have no regard to their peace and edification. Barnes notes this condemns the vain conceit of knowledge. The witnesses note Paul declaring that if any man thinks he knows anything, he knows nothing yet as he ought; the rebuke of conceited, self-confident knowledge — that the very man puffed up with his knowledge proves, by his pride and contempt of the weak, that he does not truly know, for true knowledge is humble and loving; the ignorance of the conceited. The witnesses note think that he knoweth. The witnesses note knoweth nothing as he ought.

The witnesses note the rebuke of conceited knowledge — the man puffed up with knowing proves he does not truly know; true knowledge is humble.

So the witnesses make the verse the rebuke of conceited knowledge. The lesson the expositors draw is that the man puffed up with his knowledge proves he does not truly know, for true knowledge is humble and loving. The witnesses note the conceit: "if any man think that he knoweth any thing" — Gill notes whoever is conceited with his knowledge, fancying he knows more than he does; the conceit; the self-confidence; the thinking he knows; the pride of knowledge; the inflated opinion. The expositors observe the rebuke: "he knoweth nothing yet as he ought to know" — such a man knows nothing yet as he ought; Barnes notes this condemns the vain conceit of knowledge; the rebuke; the knows nothing; the not as he ought; the ignorance; the empty knowledge. The witnesses press the proof of his ignorance: the very pride and contempt of the weak (v.1) prove that the conceited man does not truly know — for true knowledge would humble and lead him to love; the proof; the pride betraying ignorance; the contempt the evidence; the not knowing; the conceit exposed. The expositors draw the lesson on true knowledge as humble: true knowledge is humble and loving — knowing how little it knows, regarding the weak; the puffed-up knowledge is false; the true knowledge; the humility; the love; the humble knowing; the contrast. The witnesses observe the manner — "as he ought to know": the man knows nothing "as he ought to know" — not in the right manner (humbly, lovingly, practically), even if he knows the fact; the manner; the right way of knowing; the not as he ought; the humble manner; the loving knowledge. The expositors note the danger of conceited knowledge: conceited knowledge is dangerous — it puffs up, despises the weak, and is itself a mark of ignorance; the danger; the conceit; the pride; the false knowledge; the peril. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the think that he knoweth, the knoweth nothing as he ought, and the rebuke of conceited knowledge. The verse thus records the rebuke of conceited knowledge — teaching that the man puffed up with his knowledge proves he does not truly know, for true knowledge is humble and loving.
3:
"But if any man love God, the same is known of him." The witnesses note that love to God is the mark of true knowledge. Gill notes that those who love God show it by loving their brethren, being careful not to grieve them, and using their knowledge for edification, not destruction. Barnes notes there is no true knowledge not connected with love to God, which prompts a man to love his brethren. The witnesses note Paul declaring that if any man love God, the same is known of him; the contrast with the conceited knower — true religion is not in the knowledge that puffs up, but in the love to God that humbles and edifies, and the believer who loves God is known (acknowledged, approved, owned) of him; love the mark, and the being known of God the blessing. The witnesses note if any man love God. The witnesses note the same is known of him.

The witnesses note that love to God is the mark of true religion, and the one who loves God is known (owned) of him — love over mere knowledge.

So the witnesses make the verse love to God as the mark and the being known of God. The lesson the expositors draw is that true religion is not in the knowledge that puffs up, but in the love to God that humbles and is owned of him. The witnesses note the love: "if any man love God" — Gill notes those who love God show it by loving their brethren and using their knowledge for edification; the love; the loving God; the true religion; the love to God; the mark. The expositors observe the contrast with knowledge: Barnes notes there is no true knowledge not connected with love to God; the contrast; the love over knowledge; the true religion in love; the love and the knowledge; the heart over the head. The witnesses press the being known of God: "the same is known of him" — the one who loves God is known of him — acknowledged, approved, owned by God; the known of God; the owned; the approved; the acknowledged; the blessing. The expositors draw the lesson on the being known of God: to be "known" of God is to be owned, approved, loved by him — the great blessing, far above mere knowledge; the being known; the owned; the approved; the loved of God; the great privilege; "the Lord knoweth them that are his" (2 Timothy 2:19). The witnesses observe the love over the knowledge: the contrast — the conceited knower (puffed up, v.1-2) and the lover of God (humble, owned of God); the love over the knowledge; the heart over the head; the humble lover; the conceited knower; the true mark. The expositors note the true mark of religion: the true mark of religion is not knowledge (which puffs up), but love to God (which humbles and is owned of God); the true mark; the love; the religion; the heart; the love the test. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the if any man love God, the same is known of him, and love over mere knowledge. The verse thus records love to God as the mark and the being known of God — teaching that true religion is in the love to God that humbles and is owned of him, not the knowledge that puffs up.
4:
"As concerning therefore the eating of those things that are offered in sacrifice unto idols, we know that an idol is nothing in the world, and that there is none other God but one." The witnesses note the truth that an idol is nothing and God is one. Gill returns to the subject of idol-meat; Lightfoot renders "an idol is nothing in the world" — there is no such thing as the god the idol represents. The witnesses note Paul affirming, concerning idol-meat, that an idol is nothing in the world and there is no God but one; the foundational truth that grounds the believer's liberty — the idol has no real existence as a god (the heathen deity it represents is non-existent), and there is but one true God, so the meat offered to a nothing is not really defiled; the nothingness of the idol and the oneness of God. The witnesses note an idol is nothing. The witnesses note none other God but one.

The witnesses note that an idol is nothing and God is one — the foundational truth grounding the believer's liberty regarding idol-meat.

So the witnesses make the verse the nothingness of the idol and the oneness of God. The lesson the expositors draw is the foundational truth that the idol has no real existence as a god, and there is but one true God. The witnesses note the nothingness of the idol: "an idol is nothing in the world" — Lightfoot renders it: there is no such thing as the god the idol represents; the idol nothing; the no real deity; the non-existent god; the idol's nothingness; the empty idol. The expositors observe the oneness of God: "there is none other God but one" — there is but one true God; the one God; the monotheism; the only God; the oneness; the true God. The witnesses press the grounding of liberty: these truths ground the believer's liberty — the idol being nothing (no real god behind it), the meat offered to it is not really consecrated to a deity, and so not defiled; the grounding; the liberty; the idol nothing; the meat not defiled; the basis. The expositors draw the lesson on the idol's non-existence: the idol is nothing — the heathen deity it represents does not exist; the idol is wood, stone, metal, with no divinity; the non-existence; the no god behind it; the empty idol; the nothing; the false god. The witnesses observe the one true God: against the many idols, there is one true God — the Creator, the only God; the one God; the monotheism; the true God; the one; the sole deity. The expositors note the knowledge that is true: this knowledge (the idol nothing, God one) is true — and grounds the liberty; yet (v.7) not all have it, and love must guide its use; the true knowledge; the grounding; the liberty; the love guiding; the knowledge bounded. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the an idol is nothing, the none other God but one, and the foundational truth. The verse thus records the nothingness of the idol and the oneness of God — teaching the foundational truth that grounds the believer's liberty regarding idol-meat.
5:
"For though there be that are called gods, whether in heaven or in earth, (as there be gods many, and lords many,)" The witnesses note the many so-called gods of the heathen. Gill notes there are those called gods by name though not by nature — angels, magistrates, or those men account so. Barnes notes the heathen worshipped multitudes, giving them the name of gods, in heaven (the sun, moon, stars, or celestial deities) and on earth. The witnesses note Paul granting that there are many called gods, in heaven or earth — gods many and lords many; the acknowledgment of the heathen pantheon — the multitude of so-called gods and lords the pagans worshipped, named gods though not truly divine; the many idols set against the one true God; the gods many of the heathen. The witnesses note gods many, and lords many. The witnesses note called gods.

The witnesses note the many so-called gods of the heathen — "gods many, and lords many" — named gods though not truly divine.

So the witnesses make the verse the many so-called gods. The lesson the expositors draw is the acknowledgment of the heathen pantheon — the multitude of so-called gods, named gods though not truly divine. The witnesses note the called gods: "there be that are called gods" — Gill notes those called gods by name though not by nature; the called gods; the so-called; the named gods; the not truly divine; the false gods. The expositors observe the heathen worship: "whether in heaven or in earth" — Barnes notes the heathen worshipped multitudes in heaven (the sun, moon, stars, celestial deities) and on earth; the heathen worship; the gods in heaven and earth; the pantheon; the multitude; the pagan deities. The witnesses press the many: "as there be gods many, and lords many" — there are gods many and lords many (in the heathen reckoning); the many; the multitude; the gods many; the lords many; the pantheon. The expositors draw the lesson on the heathen pantheon: the heathen had a multitude of gods and lords — named gods, worshipped, but not truly divine; the pantheon; the multitude; the many gods; the false deities; the heathen worship. The witnesses observe the "called gods" not truly such: these are "called" gods — named, reckoned, worshipped as gods — but not truly divine; the so-called; the named not real; the false gods; the not truly such; the reputed deities. The expositors note the contrast with the one God: the many so-called gods are set against the one true God (v.6) — the multitude of false against the one true; the contrast; the many and the one; the false and the true; the pantheon and the one God; the set against. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the gods many and lords many, the called gods, and the heathen pantheon. The verse thus records the many so-called gods of the heathen — teaching the acknowledgment of the multitude of false gods, set against the one true God.
6:
"But to us there is but one God, the Father, of whom are all things, and we in him; and one Lord Jesus Christ, by whom are all things, and we by him." The witnesses note the great confession of the one God and one Lord. Gill notes that Christians (with the Jews and the best philosophers) hold there is but one God — clear from his perfections, his being the first cause, the governor of the world, and the Scriptures. The witnesses note Paul setting against the many idols the great Christian confession — to us there is but one God the Father, of whom are all things, and we in him; and one Lord Jesus Christ, by whom are all things, and we by him; the believer's monotheism, embracing the Father (the source of all) and the Lord Jesus Christ (the agent of all), both confessed as the one God and Lord, the creation and the believers being of the Father and by the Son; the confession of the Father and the Son. The witnesses note one God, the Father. The witnesses note one Lord Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note the great confession — one God the Father, of whom are all things, and one Lord Jesus Christ, by whom are all things.

So the witnesses make the verse the confession of the one God and one Lord. The lesson the expositors draw is the believer's monotheism, embracing the Father (the source of all) and the Lord Jesus Christ (the agent of all). The witnesses note the one God: "to us there is but one God, the Father, of whom are all things, and we in him" — there is one God, the Father, of whom (the source) are all things, and we in him; the one God; the Father; the source of all; the of whom; the monotheism. The expositors observe the one Lord: "and one Lord Jesus Christ, by whom are all things, and we by him" — one Lord Jesus Christ, by whom (the agent) are all things, and we by him; the one Lord; the Jesus Christ; the agent of all; the by whom; the Mediator. The witnesses press the confession of Father and Son: the great confession embraces the Father (the source, "of whom") and the Son (the agent, "by whom") — both confessed in the believer's monotheism; the confession; the Father and the Son; the source and the agent; the one God and Lord; the Christian creed. The expositors draw the lesson on the deity of Christ: the confession of "one Lord Jesus Christ, by whom are all things" sets Christ alongside the Father as the Creator — "by whom are all things" ascribing creation to Christ (John 1:3; Colossians 1:16); the deity of Christ; the Creator; the by whom; the divine Son; the Godhead of Christ. The witnesses observe the not-contradicting the one God: the "one Lord Jesus Christ" does not contradict the "one God" — for the Father and the Son are one God; the confession embraces both without dividing the deity; the not contradicting; the one God; the Father and Son one; the unity; the Trinitarian. The expositors note the believer's monotheism over the heathen: against the heathen's gods many and lords many (v.5), the believer has one God and one Lord; the believer's monotheism; the one over the many; the true God; the contrast; the Christian confession. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the one God the Father, the one Lord Jesus Christ, and the confession of the Father and the Son. The verse thus records the great confession of the one God and one Lord — teaching the believer's monotheism, embracing the Father the source and the Lord Jesus Christ the agent of all.
7:
"Howbeit there is not in every man that knowledge: for some with conscience of the idol unto this hour eat it as a thing offered unto an idol; and their conscience being weak is defiled." The witnesses note that not all have this knowledge, and the weak conscience defiled. Gill notes Paul speaks not of heathens but of Christians, in whom there was the knowledge of these things, but not in all — some, with a conscience of the idol, still ate the meat as offered to a real idol, and their weak conscience was defiled. The witnesses note Paul observing that not every believer has this knowledge — some, with a lingering sense of the idol's reality, eat the meat as truly offered to an idol, and so their weak conscience is defiled; the weaker brother whose conscience is not yet clear, who, eating against his scruple, is defiled — not by the meat itself, but by acting against his conscience; the defiled weak conscience. The witnesses note not in every man that knowledge. The witnesses note their conscience being weak is defiled.

The witnesses note that not all have this knowledge — the weak, eating against conscience, are defiled; not by the meat, but by acting against scruple.

So the witnesses make the verse the weak conscience defiled. The lesson the expositors draw is that the weaker brother, whose conscience is not clear, is defiled by eating against his scruple — not by the meat itself, but by acting against conscience. The witnesses note the lack of knowledge: "there is not in every man that knowledge" — Gill notes Paul speaks of Christians, not all of whom have this knowledge; the not all; the lack; the not every man; the knowledge wanting; the weak. The expositors observe the lingering sense: "some with conscience of the idol unto this hour eat it as a thing offered unto an idol" — some, with a lingering conscience (sense) of the idol's reality, eat the meat as truly offered to an idol; the lingering sense; the conscience of the idol; the eating as offered; the weak scruple; the half-belief. The witnesses press the defilement: "their conscience being weak is defiled" — their weak conscience, eating against its scruple, is defiled; the defilement; the weak conscience; the defiled; the conscience polluted; the harm. The expositors draw the lesson on defilement by acting against conscience: the weak are defiled not by the meat itself (which is nothing, v.4), but by acting against their conscience — eating what they still scruple; the defilement; the not the meat; the against conscience; the scruple violated; the conscience wounded. The witnesses observe the weaker brother: the weaker brother is one whose conscience is not yet clear — who still half-believes the idol real, and so cannot eat with a free conscience; the weaker brother; the weak conscience; the unclear; the scruple; the not free. The expositors note the principle of conscience: the principle — to act against one's conscience (even a mistaken one) is sin, for it is to do what one believes wrong; the principle; the conscience; the acting against; the sin; the conscience to be respected (Romans 14:23). The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not in every man that knowledge, the conscience being weak is defiled, and the weaker brother. The verse thus records the weak conscience defiled — teaching that the weaker brother is defiled by eating against his scruple, not by the meat itself, but by acting against conscience.
8:
"But meat commendeth us not to God: for neither, if we eat, are we the better; neither, if we eat not, are we the worse." The witnesses note that food does not commend us to God. Gill notes the words may be the argument of those with knowledge (that eating is indifferent, neither meritorious nor blameworthy), or Paul's own sense — that eating or not eating makes a man neither better nor worse before God. The witnesses note Paul stating that meat does not commend us to God — we are neither the better for eating nor the worse for not eating; the indifference of food in itself — that eating or abstaining from idol-meat (or any meat) does not affect one's standing with God, being a thing morally indifferent; the principle that the meat itself is neither here nor there, setting up the deeper concern of the weaker brother; the indifference of food. The witnesses note meat commendeth us not to God. The witnesses note neither better nor worse.

The witnesses note that food does not commend us to God — neither better for eating nor worse for not eating; the indifference of food in itself.

So the witnesses make the verse the indifference of food. The lesson the expositors draw is that eating or abstaining does not affect one's standing with God, the meat itself being morally indifferent. The witnesses note the principle: "meat commendeth us not to God" — Gill notes the words may be the argument of those with knowledge, or Paul's own sense; meat does not commend us to God; the principle; the food indifferent; the not commending; the no merit; the indifference. The expositors observe the two sides: "neither, if we eat, are we the better; neither, if we eat not, are we the worse" — neither the better for eating nor the worse for not eating; the two sides; the eating and not eating; the no difference; the indifference; the neither way. The witnesses press the indifference of food: the meat itself is morally indifferent — eating or abstaining does not affect one's standing with God; the indifference; the morally neutral; the food neither here nor there; the no moral weight; the indifferent matter. The expositors draw the lesson on the food not affecting standing: food does not commend or condemn before God — it is the heart, the faith, the love that matter, not the meat; the not affecting standing; the heart over the food; the no merit in eating or abstaining; the indifference; the spiritual over the material. The witnesses observe the setting up of the deeper concern: the indifference of the food sets up the deeper concern — the weaker brother (v.9-13); since the meat is indifferent, the believer can well forgo it for the brother's sake; the setting up; the deeper concern; the weaker brother; the liberty to forgo; the love. The expositors note the balance: the meat is indifferent (so the strong may eat), but the brother is not indifferent (so the strong should forgo for him); the balance; the food and the brother; the liberty and the love; the indifferent meat, the precious brother; the two. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the meat commendeth us not to God, the neither better nor worse, and the indifference of food. The verse thus records that food does not commend us to God — teaching the indifference of food in itself, setting up the deeper concern of the weaker brother.
9:
"But take heed lest by any means this liberty of yours become a stumblingblock to them that are weak." The witnesses note the warning that liberty may become a stumbling-block. Barnes notes this is the reply to the Corinthians' argument (v.8) — though all you say be true, yet take heed; the liberty must not be used to the harm of the weak. The witnesses note Paul warning that, though the food is indifferent, the believer must take heed lest his liberty become a stumbling-block to the weak; the bounding of liberty by love — that the right to eat (real and lawful) must yield where its use would cause a weaker brother to stumble (be emboldened to sin against his conscience); the liberty bounded by the brother's good. The witnesses note take heed. The witnesses note a stumblingblock to the weak.

The witnesses note the warning that liberty may become a stumbling-block to the weak — liberty bounded by the brother's good.

So the witnesses make the verse the warning against liberty becoming a stumbling-block. The lesson the expositors draw is that the right to eat must yield where its use would cause a weaker brother to stumble. The witnesses note the warning: "take heed" — Barnes notes this is the reply to the Corinthians' argument (v.8) — though all you say be true, yet take heed; the warning; the caution; the take heed; the watch; the heed. The expositors observe the liberty: "this liberty of yours" — the believer's liberty (the right to eat the indifferent meat) is real and lawful; the liberty; the right; the freedom; the lawful liberty; the real right. The witnesses press the stumbling-block: "become a stumblingblock to them that are weak" — the liberty must not become a stumbling-block (an occasion of falling) to the weak; the stumbling-block; the occasion of falling; the harm to the weak; the offense; the cause of stumbling. The expositors draw the lesson on bounding liberty by love: the liberty must yield where its use would cause a weaker brother to stumble — to be emboldened to sin against his conscience (v.10); the bounding; the liberty yielding; the love over the right; the brother's good; the liberty limited. The witnesses observe the real but limited right: the right to eat is real (the meat indifferent), but limited by love — the believer is free to eat, yet should forgo where it harms the weak; the real right; the limited; the liberty and the love; the free yet yielding; the bounded liberty. The expositors note the responsibility for the weak: the strong are responsible for the weak — their liberty must not harm them; the responsibility; the care for the weak; the not harming; the brother's keeper; the love's duty. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the take heed, the stumblingblock to the weak, and the liberty bounded by love. The verse thus records the warning against liberty becoming a stumbling-block — teaching that the right to eat must yield where its use would cause a weaker brother to stumble.
10:
"For if any man see thee which hast knowledge sit at meat in the idol's temple, shall not the conscience of him which is weak be emboldened to eat those things which are offered to idols;" The witnesses note the danger of the example to the weak. Gill notes that if a weak believer (not one of equal knowledge) sees one famed for knowledge sitting at meat in the idol's temple, his conscience may be emboldened. The witnesses note Paul illustrating the danger — if the weak brother sees the knowledgeable believer feasting in the idol's temple, his conscience will be emboldened (encouraged) to eat the idol-meat against his scruple; the peril of the bad example — the strong, by his liberty openly used, leading the weak to act against his conscience, and so to sin; the emboldening of the weak. The witnesses note sit at meat in the idol's temple. The witnesses note the conscience be emboldened.

The witnesses note the danger of the example — the weak, seeing the knowledgeable feast in the idol's temple, emboldened to eat against his conscience.

So the witnesses make the verse the danger of the example to the weak. The lesson the expositors draw is the peril of the bad example, the strong leading the weak to act against his conscience. The witnesses note the example: "if any man see thee which hast knowledge sit at meat in the idol's temple" — Gill notes the weak believer sees one famed for knowledge feasting in the idol's temple; the example; the seeing; the knowledgeable feasting; the idol's temple; the open liberty. The expositors observe the emboldening: "shall not the conscience of him which is weak be emboldened" — the weak brother's conscience will be emboldened (encouraged, led) to do the same; the emboldening; the encouraged; the led; the conscience moved; the influence. The witnesses press the eating against conscience: "to eat those things which are offered to idols" — the weak, emboldened, eats the idol-meat against his scruple; the eating; the against conscience; the idol-meat; the scruple violated; the harm. The expositors draw the lesson on the peril of the bad example: the strong, by openly using his liberty (feasting in the temple), leads the weak to act against his conscience — a peril; the peril; the bad example; the leading astray; the influence; the harm by example. The witnesses observe the influence of the strong: the strong has influence over the weak — his example emboldens, leads; therefore he must use his liberty with care; the influence; the example's power; the leading; the responsibility; the care needed. The expositors note the weak led to sin: the weak, eating against his conscience, sins — for to act against conscience is sin (Romans 14:23); the weak led to sin; the conscience violated; the sin; the harm; the stumbling. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the sit at meat in the idol's temple, the conscience be emboldened, and the danger of the example. The verse thus records the danger of the example to the weak — teaching the peril of the bad example, the strong leading the weak to act against his conscience.
11:
"And through thy knowledge shall the weak brother perish, for whom Christ died?" The witnesses note the grave warning — the weak brother perishing. Gill notes this aggravates the sin of those who, by their example, ensnare weak minds, causing them to stumble and fall, even in some sense so as to perish. Barnes notes you will thus be the means of deceiving and destroying him. The witnesses note Paul warning that, through the strong man's knowledge (ill-used), the weak brother — for whom Christ died — may perish; the gravity of harming the weak, set in the most solemn terms — that the careless use of liberty may destroy one whom Christ loved enough to die for; the witnesses note the debate on "perish": whether the weak truly finally perish (the danger real, the Arminian reading), or whether the sense is the wounding and tendency toward ruin (the Reformed reading, the elect not finally lost, yet the sin and the danger real); the weak brother and the death of Christ. The witnesses note the weak brother perish. The witnesses note for whom Christ died.

The witnesses note the grave warning — the weak brother, for whom Christ died, may perish through the careless use of liberty; the gravity of harming the weak.

So the witnesses make the verse the grave warning of the weak brother perishing. The lesson the expositors draw is the gravity of harming one whom Christ loved enough to die for. The witnesses note the harm: "through thy knowledge shall the weak brother perish" — Gill notes this aggravates the sin of ensnaring weak minds; Barnes notes the means of destroying him; the harm; the weak perishing; the through thy knowledge; the destruction; the ruin. The expositors observe the aggravation — "for whom Christ died": "for whom Christ died" — the weak brother is one for whom Christ died; the aggravation; the Christ died; the precious brother; the loved of Christ; the bought with blood. The witnesses press the gravity: the careless use of liberty may destroy one whom Christ loved enough to die for — the gravity; the harming the precious; the destroying the loved; the careless liberty; the grave sin. The expositors present the debate on "perish": the witnesses note the debate — the Arminian reads it that the weak may truly, finally perish (the danger real, the apostasy possible); the Reformed reads it that the sense is the wounding, the offense, the tendency toward ruin (the elect not finally lost, yet the sin and the danger real, the strong guilty as if he destroyed him); both hold the warning solemn and the sin grave. The witnesses draw the lesson on the value of the brother: the brother is of great value — one for whom Christ died; to harm him is to despise Christ's love; the value; the precious brother; the Christ's love; the not to be harmed; the worth. The expositors note the warning to the strong: the warning is to the strong — your liberty must not destroy the brother Christ died for; the warning; the strong cautioned; the liberty bounded; the brother spared; the love. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the weak brother perish, the for whom Christ died, and the gravity of harming the weak. The verse thus records the grave warning of the weak brother perishing — teaching the gravity of harming one whom Christ died for through the careless use of liberty.
12:
"But when ye sin so against the brethren, and wound their weak conscience, ye sin against Christ." The witnesses note that sinning against the brethren is sinning against Christ. Gill notes that, by sitting at meat in an idol's temple and violating the new commandment of love, the believer wounds the weak brother's conscience and sins against the brethren. Barnes notes this shows the evil of causing others to sin. The witnesses note Paul declaring that when they so sin against the brethren and wound their weak conscience, they sin against Christ; the solemn truth that to harm a fellow-believer (to wound his conscience, lead him to sin) is to sin against Christ himself — for the brethren are members of Christ, united to him, so that what is done against them is done against him; sinning against Christ in the brethren. The witnesses note wound their weak conscience. The witnesses note ye sin against Christ.

The witnesses note that wounding the weak brother's conscience is sinning against Christ himself — for the brethren are members of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse sinning against the brethren as sinning against Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that to harm a fellow-believer is to sin against Christ himself, for the brethren are members of Christ. The witnesses note the sin against the brethren: "when ye sin so against the brethren" — Gill notes that, by feasting in the idol's temple and violating the law of love, the believer sins against the brethren; the sin against the brethren; the harming the brother; the against the brethren; the offense; the wrong. The expositors observe the wounding: "and wound their weak conscience" — the believer wounds the weak brother's conscience — leading him to sin against it, defiling it; the wounding; the weak conscience; the harm; the conscience wounded; the injury. The witnesses press the sin against Christ: "ye sin against Christ" — to so sin against the brethren is to sin against Christ; the sin against Christ; the against the Lord; the Christ harmed; the solemn truth; the offense to Christ. The expositors draw the lesson on the union of Christ and the brethren: the brethren are members of Christ, united to him — so that what is done against them is done against him; the union; the members of Christ; the done to him; the Christ in the brethren; "inasmuch as ye have done it unto one of the least of these... ye have done it unto me" (Matthew 25:40); the union. The witnesses observe the gravity raised to the highest: the gravity of harming the weak is raised to the highest — it is sin against Christ himself; the gravity; the highest; the against Christ; the solemn; the supreme offense. The expositors note the motive against careless liberty: the truth — that harming the brother is sinning against Christ — is the supreme motive against the careless use of liberty; the motive; the against careless liberty; the love for Christ; the sparing the brother; the supreme reason. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the wound their weak conscience, the ye sin against Christ, and the union of Christ and the brethren. The verse thus records sinning against the brethren as sinning against Christ — teaching that to harm a fellow-believer is to sin against Christ himself, for the brethren are members of Christ.
13:
"Wherefore, if meat make my brother to offend, I will eat no flesh while the world standeth, lest I make my brother to offend." The witnesses note Paul's resolve to forgo his liberty for the brother. Gill notes the conclusion Paul makes for himself, and proposes for the imitation of others — that since the imprudent use of liberty might lay a stumbling-block before weak Christians, he would forgo it. The witnesses note Paul resolving that, if meat make his brother to offend, he will eat no flesh while the world stands, rather than cause his brother to stumble; the supreme example of self-denying love — the apostle willing to give up forever a lawful liberty (eating meat) rather than harm a weaker brother; the love that gladly forgoes the lawful for the brother's sake; the self-denial of love. The witnesses note I will eat no flesh. The witnesses note lest I make my brother to offend.

The witnesses note Paul's resolve to forgo meat forever rather than cause his brother to stumble — the supreme example of self-denying love.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's resolve to forgo his liberty. The lesson the expositors draw is the supreme example of self-denying love, the apostle willing to give up a lawful liberty forever for the brother's sake. The witnesses note the condition: "if meat make my brother to offend" — Gill notes the conclusion Paul makes — if meat makes his brother offend (stumble); the condition; the if meat; the brother offended; the stumbling; the harm. The expositors observe the resolve: "I will eat no flesh while the world standeth" — Paul resolves to eat no flesh forever (while the world stands) rather than harm his brother; the resolve; the eat no flesh; the forever; the giving up; the self-denial. The witnesses press the lawful liberty forgone: Paul would forgo a lawful liberty (eating meat, which is indifferent, v.8) — not because it is wrong, but for the brother's sake; the lawful forgone; the liberty given up; the indifferent surrendered; the right yielded; the love over the right. The expositors draw the lesson on self-denying love: Paul's resolve is the supreme example of self-denying love — willing to give up forever a lawful liberty rather than harm a weaker brother; the self-denying love; the supreme example; the giving up for the brother; the love that forgoes; the sacrifice. The witnesses observe the example for imitation: Paul proposes this for the imitation of others — the strong to forgo their liberty for the weak; the example; the imitation; the model; the strong to follow; the pattern of love. The expositors note the purpose — "lest I make my brother to offend": the purpose is the brother's good — lest he be made to stumble; the purpose; the brother spared; the lest he offend; the love's aim; the brother's good. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I will eat no flesh, the lest I make my brother to offend, and the self-denial of love. The verse thus records Paul's resolve to forgo his liberty — teaching the supreme example of self-denying love, the apostle willing to give up a lawful liberty forever for the brother's sake.
